Shannon Diversity Analsysis

#Load packages used for analysis 

library(vegan)

library(reshape2)

The following object is masked from ‘package:tidyr’:

    smiths

Step 1 Load phyloseq object and prep data for phyloseq

```r
```r

#Manually ewww...(we will write a function to automate this)

data_phyla = sample_data(physeq_phyla)
#rownames(data_phyla)
merged_data_phyla = merge(data_phyla,alpha_div_phyla, by = \row.names\)

data_OTU = sample_data(physeq_otu)
merged_data_OTU = merge(data_OTU,alpha_div_fam, by = \row.names\)

data_family = sample_data(physeq_family)
merged_data_OTU = merge(data_family,alpha_div_fam, by = \row.names\)

Preparing data:

physeq_filtered = subset_samples(ps.merged, Soil_inoculation != "Sterile" & Response != "NA")

physeq_filtered<- prune_species(speciesSums(physeq_filtered) > 0, physeq_filtered)

Warning: 'prune_species' is deprecated.
Use 'prune_taxa' instead.
See help("Deprecated") and help("phyloseq-deprecated").
Warning: 'speciesSums' is deprecated.
Use 'taxa_sums' instead.
See help("Deprecated") and help("phyloseq-deprecated").

function we used is “deprecated” meaning its dated function…but its okay…

psdata_phyla = tax_glom(physeq_trimmed,taxrank=rank_names(physeq_trimmed)[2])
alpha_div = estimate_richness(psdata_phyla,measures = c("Shannon","Chao1", "Simpson"))

Warning in estimate_richness(psdata_phyla, measures = c("Shannon", "Chao1",  :
  The data you have provided does not have
any singletons. This is highly suspicious. Results of richness
estimates (for example) are probably unreliable, or wrong, if you have already
trimmed low-abundance taxa from the data.

We recommended that you find the un-trimmed data and retry.

Let’s explore diversity at multiple levels (Phyla, Family, ASV) between each Clade: Shannon = evenness and richness are equally taken into consideration Chao1 = Richness plays a larger role giving greater emphasis

Step 2 Generating alpha diversity indices

Diversity Indexes Shannon: Equally balances evenness and richness in calculating a value Chao1: Tends to favor more rare taxa (greater emphasis on richness) Simpson: Favors more dominant taxa more (greater emphasis on evenness)

Now let’s try running all this as a function since we will be doing this alot:

merged_data_Phyla1 = alpha_div_f(physeq_trimmed,2)

Warning in estimate_richness(psdata, measures = c("Shannon", "Simpson")) :
  The data you have provided does not have
any singletons. This is highly suspicious. Results of richness
estimates (for example) are probably unreliable, or wrong, if you have already
trimmed low-abundance taxa from the data.

We recommended that you find the un-trimmed data and retry.
rank_names(merged_data_ASV)
Error in rank_names(merged_data_ASV) : 
  could not find function "rank_names"

Step 3 Modeling alpha diversity

Question 1: Does alpha diversity differ between Clades? Yes but its not very statistically significant…

Based on our beta diversity data, we saw that Clade was a major indicator of variance at iteration 3 on wards.

#ASV level

CHAO1 diversity (richness)

mean_div_ASV = merged_data_ASV %>%
  group_by(Clade) %>%
  summarize(avg_shannon = mean(Shannon), avg_Chao1 = mean(Chao1), avg_Simpsom = mean(Simpson))
Error in group_by(., Clade) : object 'merged_data_ASV' not found

Model for Chao1

library(lmerTest)
library(lme4)

model_Chao1 = lmer(Chao1 ~ Clade * Soil_type.x * Selection.x + (1|Block), data = subset(merged_data_ASV, Iteration == 5))
anova(model_Chao1)

model_Chao1 = lmer(Chao1 ~ Clade * Soil_type.x * Selection.x *Iteration + (1|Block), data = merged_data_ASV)
anova(model_Chao1)

summary(model_Chao1)

Conclusions (Chao1 index): We see that overall ASV richness was varied overtime the variation seems to have increased…So it looks like looking at species richness we were able to generate variation from our soil selection experiment but that was only realizable at iteration 5. Variation was greater in moderns that in Andigenum and lesser extent in Chiletanum landraces.

Clade:Iteration effect linked to alpha diversity \(p = 1.951 \times 10^{-8}\) *** But also there’s a lot of randomness wrapped in that I guess.

IDK: Should we also do the estimated marginal means as well for alpha diversity or not? and plot it out?

Shannon Diversity

ggplot_shannon = ggplot(merged_data_ASV, aes(x = Clade, y = Shannon, fill = Clade))+
  geom_dotplot(binaxis = "y", stackdir = "center", alpha = 2)+
  theme_bw()+
  facet_wrap(~Iteration)
ggplot_shannon
Bin width defaults to 1/30 of the range of the data. Pick better value with `binwidth`.

Conclusions(Shannon diversity): Interesting trends in that Shannon diversity we see tighter clustering of modern Clade samples that are increasing in diversity. While Chiletanum is following a similar trend but is more varied. Andigenum shannon diversity is overall pretty consistent excpet for the last iteration…

Model for Shannon diversity

library(lme4)
library(lmerTest)
library(emmeans)
Warning: package ‘emmeans’ was built under R version 4.1.2
model_shannon = lmer(Shannon ~ Clade * Soil_type.x * Selection.x + (1|Block), data = subset(merged_data_ASV, Iteration == 5))
anova(model_shannon)

model_shannon = lmer(Shannon ~ Clade * Soil_type.x * Selection.x * Iteration + (1|Block), data = merged_data_ASV)
fixed-effect model matrix is rank deficient so dropping 6 columns / coefficients

Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Based on our statistical test Shannon index was weakly linked to Clade…\(p-value\) = 0.096

ggplot_simpson = ggplot(merged_data_ASV, aes(x = Clade, y = Simpson, fill = Clade))+
  geom_dotplot(binaxis = "y", stackdir = "center", alpha = 2)+
  theme_bw()+
  facet_wrap(~Iteration)
ggplot_simpson
Bin width defaults to 1/30 of the range of the data. Pick better value with `binwidth`.

Conclusions: hard to see any effect from Clade on the alpha diversity based on the Simpson index…

Let’s write a function for graphing our data:

ggplot_alpha_div = function(merged_data, alpha_div){
  ggplot(merged_data, aes(x = Clade, y = alpha_div, fill = Clade))+
  geom_dotplot(binaxis = "y", stackdir = "center", alpha = 2)+
  theme_bw()+
  facet_wrap(~Iteration)
}

#hmmm not working....

#Alpha diversity at Phyla level

Simpson diversity

ggplot_simpson = ggplot(merged_data_phyla, aes(x = Clade, y = Simpson, fill = Clade))+
  geom_dotplot(binaxis = "y", stackdir = "center", alpha = 2)+
  theme_bw()+
  facet_wrap(~Iteration)
ggplot_simpson
Bin width defaults to 1/30 of the range of the data. Pick better value with `binwidth`.

Soil_type.x:Selection.x:Iteration        0.6522   0.42000    
Clade:Soil_type.x:Selection.x:Iteration  0.1280   0.87994    
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

#Alpha Diversity at Family level:

ggplot_shannon = ggplot(merged_data_fam, aes(x = Clade, y = Shannon, fill = Clade))+
  geom_dotplot(binaxis = "y", stackdir = "center", alpha = 2)+
  theme_bw()+
  facet_wrap(~Iteration)
ggplot_shannon
Bin width defaults to 1/30 of the range of the data. Pick better value with `binwidth`.

Model

lmerModLmerTest]

Use print(x, correlation=TRUE)  or
    vcov(x)        if you need it
optimizer (nloptwrap) convergence code: 0 (OK)
boundary (singular) fit: see ?isSingular

No Clade effect, instead see Selection.x and Iteration effect. See that Positive selection increases alpha diveristy (measured by Shannon index) at the family level.

ggplot_simpson = ggplot(merged_data_fam, aes(x = Clade, y = Simpson, fill = Clade))+
  geom_dotplot(binaxis = "y", stackdir = "center", alpha = 2)+
  theme_bw()+
  facet_wrap(~Iteration)
ggplot_simpson
Bin width defaults to 1/30 of the range of the data. Pick better value with `binwidth`.

Model

model_simpsom = lmer(Simpson ~ Clade * Soil_type.x * Selection.x * Iteration + (1|Block), data = subset(merged_data_fam, Selection.x != "none"))
anova(model_simpsom)

Iteration is the only thing making a difference at in terms of alpha diversity at the family level…for Simpson’s.
